definition: Ambient air pollution refers to a mixture of particles and gases in outdoor air. In GENARCH v1.0 seed content, this page focuses on fine particulate matter (PM2.5: particles ≤ 2.5 μm in diameter), a widely measured proxy associated with respiratory and cardiovascular health outcomes at the population level. PM2.5 is measured in micrograms per cubic meter (μg/m³) using regulatory monitoring networks and modeled surfaces.
name: Ambient Air Pollution (PM2.5)

proxies.measurement_method: Regulatory monitors + modeled estimates (annual or daily averages)
proxies.name: PM2.5

references.title: Outdoor air pollution and asthma in children

references.journal: Journal of the Air & Waste Management Association
references.title: Air pollution and health: evidence and mechanisms (review)
IL-33 and the biology of the epithelial barrier in allergic inflammation
The GTEx Consortium atlas of genetic regulatory effects across human tissues (v8)

sensitive_windows.mechanism_rationale: Airway and immune development occur during gestation; exposures during this window may have durable population-level effects on later respiratory outcomes.
Rapid lung growth and developing immune responses can make early-life exposures disproportionately influential in cohort studies.
Ongoing airway maturation and changing activity patterns can shape exposure-dose and inflammatory responses in population studies.

systems_affected.mechanism_summary: Particles deposit along the airway surface, promoting oxidative stress, epithelial injury, and inflammatory signaling that can worsen airway hyperreactivity in susceptible populations.
Exposure can shift immune signaling toward pro-inflammatory programs and alter barrier-immune interactions (context dependent).
Systemic inflammation and endothelial activation are hypothesized pathways linking PM2.5 to cardiovascular burden.
